why: Everyday spoken word in urban fashion talk; native पहनावा/உடை exist but 'outfit' is what people actually say for a put-together look.
Used as-is for both the furniture and one's clothing collection; अलमारी covers only the cupboard, not the collection sense.
Standard spoken fashion term; no concise native word covers bags/jewellery/belts as one category.
Genuine everyday native words: kapdaa (Hindi) and thuni (Tamil) are the natural way to refer to cloth/material.
Silaai and thaiyal are the natural, universally used words for stitching/sewing.
Hindi kadhaai is the genuine everyday word; Tamil commonly keeps the loan 'embroidery' in speech (நூல் வேலை is the native alternative).
Fashion-technical term; a literal 'shadow shape / परछाई आकार' is absurd and loses the garment-outline meaning.
Style label with a desirable retro/period connotation; literal 'पुराना/old' loses the classic-aesthetic nuance.
Used directly in everyday dress-code talk; no natural one-word native equivalent in the fashion register.
Everyday loanword for dress code; native औपचारिक/முறையான sound bureaucratic and aren't used for clothing.
Common spoken word; native phrasing like चलन में is stiff and not how people describe fashionable items.
Tamil oora-vaithal (to steep/soak in spices) is the genuine native cooking term; Hindi naturally describes it as steeping in spices.
Natural native verbs for topping/decorating a dish: sajaana (Hindi) and alangarithal (Tamil).
Real everyday cooking verbs: phentnaa (Hindi, to beat/whisk) and kadaithal (Tamil, to beat).
Genuine native words: Hindi ghol (a pourable mix) and Tamil maavu (batter, as in dosa maavu).
Described naturally as 'cook on low flame'; this is the actual instruction Indian cooks use, no loanword needed.
Native compounds for 'heat beforehand' are natural and clear in oven instructions.
Hindi aata / guundha hua aata and Tamil maavu are the genuine everyday words for kneaded dough.
Western-baking product term (interchangeable with icing); no native equivalent and a literal 'cover/परत' translation is misleading.
Genuine native umbrella words: Hindi peya/pey-padaarth and Tamil paanam naturally mean a drink.
Hindi menu speech uses 'starter' (accepted loan); Tamil thodakka-unavu (starting food) works natively. Formal क्षुधावर्धक is too literary.
Natural native phrasing for 'remaining food': bacha hua khaana (Hindi) and meedham ulla unavu (Tamil).
Native travel-plan terms yatra-karyakram (Hindi) and payana-thittam (Tamil) are genuine and understood; the English word is hard for general audiences.
Natural native expressions: thaharne ki vyavastha / rehne ka intezaam (Hindi) and thangumidam (Tamil) are everyday travel words.
Native keepsake words: Hindi yaadgaar/nishaani and Tamil ninaivup-parisu (memory gift) convey it genuinely and avoid the hard French loan.
Genuine native scenery words: Hindi nazaara (view/scene) and Tamil nilakkaatchi (land-view).
Specific budget/independent-travel concept; a literal 'carrying a bag on the back' misses the meaning, so kept as the loan in travel content.
Native list words soochi/jaanch-soochi (Hindi) and pattiyal (Tamil) are clear and natural.
Common spoken loanword in home/interior contexts; native सजावट/அலங்காரம் are also natural but 'decor' is the prevailing register word.
Fixed airline-document term; any literal translation is absurd and unrecognizable at the airport. Universally said in English.
Genuine native words: Hindi vidhi/pakaane ki vidhi/nuskha and Tamil seimurai (method) are standard in cooking content.
Native words saamagri (Hindi) and thevaiyaana porutkal (Tamil) appear in every recipe in both languages.
Everyday loanword; native भोजनालय sounds archaic and is not used in casual speech.
Fully naturalized everyday word (in Tamil it also colloquially means eatery). No native substitute is used.
Long-naturalized everyday word for travel/entry; no native equivalent is in spoken use.
Established everyday loanword; native phrasing for 'trend in dress' doesn't carry the style connotation people mean.
Fully naturalized casual word ('uska style accha hai'); native शैली/பாணி are literary and not used conversationally.
Everyday loanword for the baked item; no native word exists or is sought.
Fixed category label; literal सड़क का खाना reads oddly while the English term is the marketing-standard phrase now widely used.
Fixed product/category term; a literal 'तेज़ खाना/fast (speed) food' is absurd. Universally kept in English.
Genuine everyday occupational words: Hindi darzi and Tamil thaiyalkaarar are exactly what people call a tailor.
